ui.heroTitle: Học Rust qua những câu đố
ui.heroBody: Đi qua từng bài học ngắn. Mỗi bài chạy code Rust thật và yêu cầu bạn đoán output hoặc chọn đáp án đúng. Miễn phí, không cần tài khoản, ngay trên trình duyệt.

01_hello.title: Hello, world!
01_hello.description: Chương trình Rust đầu tiên của bạn. Macro println! in ra standard output. Bạn có thể chèn biến trực tiếp vào trong format string.

q1.question: Chương trình này in ra gì? (Hãy nhập output chính xác.)

q2.question: println! là gì?
options: Một hàm
Một macro
Một trait
Một từ khóa

q3.question: In `println!("Hello, {name}!")`, what does `{name}` do?
options: Creates a new variable called `name`
Prints the braces literally
Inserts the value of `name` into the output
Calls a method on `name`

q4.question: Why does `println!` end with `!` in Rust?
options: Because it is a macro invocation
Because it mutates global output state
Because it returns `!`
Because all printing syntax uses punctuation

q5.question: If a program calls `println!("Hello");` and then `println!("Rust");`, what is printed?
options: `Hello Rust` on one line
`Hello` and `Rust` on separate lines
Only `Rust`
Nothing until `flush()` is called

02_variables.title: Biến và tính có thể thay đổi
02_variables.description: Trong Rust, biến mặc định là bất biến. Thêm `mut` để cho phép gán lại. Việc khai báo kiểu là không bắt buộc khi trình biên dịch có thể suy luận.

q1.question: Hãy đoán output của chương trình:

q2.question: Sau `let y: i32 = 10;` (không có mut), dòng nào sẽ vẫn biên dịch được nếu thêm vào sau đó?
options: let y = y + 1;
y.set(11);
explanations: This tries to assign a new value to `y`, but `y` is immutable because it was not declared with `mut`.
This is also a mutation. `+=` still changes the existing variable, so it needs `mut`.
This works because it creates a new variable named `y` that shadows the old one instead of mutating it.
`i32` does not have a `.set(...)` method, so this fails before mutability even matters.
q2.answerReason: The correct choice is `let y = y + 1;` because it shadows the original binding with a new one.

q3.question: What is happening in `let y = y + 1;`?
options: The old `y` is mutated in place
A new `y` shadows the old one
Rust automatically adds `mut`
The type of `y` changes to `usize`

q4.question: When is an explicit type annotation like `let y: i32 = 10;` most useful?
options: When you need to guide the compiler or document the intended type
When you want to make a variable mutable
When you want Rust to skip type checking
When shadowing is not allowed

q5.question: What is the key difference between mutation and shadowing in Rust?
options: Mutation creates a new binding, while shadowing edits the old one
Shadowing creates a new binding, while mutation changes an existing one
They are identical except for style
Mutation works only for numbers, shadowing only for strings

03_ownership.title: Ownership và borrowing
03_ownership.description: Mỗi giá trị trong Rust có đúng một chủ sở hữu. Truyền giá trị qua reference (&) là mượn nó mà không lấy ownership. Khi một giá trị đã được move, binding gốc không còn dùng được nữa.

q1.question: Hãy đoán output của chương trình:

q2.question: Sau khi gọi `takes_ownership(owned)`, điều gì xảy ra nếu thử dùng `owned` lần nữa?
options: Lỗi biên dịch: giá trị đã được move
Panic lúc chạy
In ra chuỗi rỗng
Vẫn chạy bình thường

q3.question: Which argument style borrows a value instead of moving it?
options: `String`
`Vec<i32>`
`Box<T>`
`&String`

q4.question: Why would a function take `&String` instead of `String` when reading text?
options: To borrow the value without taking ownership
To force Rust to copy the heap allocation
Because `String` cannot be passed to functions
To convert the value into a trait object

q5.question: If a function needs to modify a caller's `String` in place, which parameter type best communicates that intent?
options: `String`
`&String`
`&mut String`
`&&String`

04_control_flow.title: Control flow

q2.question: How many times does `for x in 0..3` iterate?
options: 0 times
2 times
3 times
4 times

q3.question: Which range includes the final value `3`?

q4.question: Why does the range `0..3` stop before `3`?
options: Because `..` means an inclusive range
Because the upper bound of `a..b` is exclusive
Because loops in Rust always skip the last item
Because `3` is reserved for indexing

q5.question: When is a `while` loop usually the better fit than `for x in 0..n`?
options: When the number of iterations depends on a changing condition
When you want Rust to unroll the loop
When iterating over a fixed range
When you need pattern matching

05_functions.title: Functions and expressions

q2.question: Why does `fn add(a, b) -> i32 { a + b }` return `a + b` without a `return` keyword?
options: All Rust functions auto-return their last variable
The last expression without a semicolon is the block's value
`return` is illegal in Rust
The compiler inserts a return at the end

q3.question: What happens if you add a semicolon after `a + b` in that function?
options: The function returns `a + b` faster
Rust ignores the semicolon
The block no longer returns `a + b` as its value
The return type changes to `bool`

q4.question: Why must Rust function parameters include their types in the signature?
options: So macros can rewrite the function body
So the compiler knows the contract at compile time
So runtime reflection can inspect them
So parameters can be mutated automatically

q5.question: What value does a block evaluate to when its last line ends with a semicolon?
options: The last expression's value
The block's line count
The unit value `()`
A hidden `Result`

06_strings.title: Strings: String vs &str

q2.question: Which statement is true?
options: &str owns its data
String is heap-allocated and growable
String is always shorter than &str
&str cannot hold UTF-8

q3.question: Which type can grow with methods like `push`?
options: `String`
`&str`
`char`
`usize`

q4.question: Why do many APIs prefer taking `&str` instead of `String` for text input?
options: `&str` guarantees ASCII only
`&str` lets callers pass borrowed text without transferring ownership
`String` cannot be read by functions
`&str` always allocates less memory at runtime

q5.question: What does `format!(...)` produce?
options: A borrowed `&str` tied to the current scope
A new owned `String` built from the formatted data
A `char` array on the stack
It prints directly to stdout and returns `()`

07_tuples_arrays.title: Tuples and arrays

q2.question: Fill in the blank to destructure the tuple.

q3.question: Which statement about arrays is true?
options: An array can mix `i32` and `&str` freely
All array elements must have the same type
Array length is decided only at runtime
Arrays cannot be indexed

q4.question: Why is array length considered part of the type in Rust?
options: So `[i32; 3]` and `[i32; 4]` are treated as different fixed-size types
So arrays can store mixed types
So array indexing becomes optional
So arrays can only live on the heap

q5.question: When is a tuple usually a better fit than an array?
options: When you need a growable sequence
When you need to store values of different types together
When every element must share one type
When you need to sort values in place

08_enums.title: Enums and match
08_enums.description:
An enum lets one value have several possible shapes.

```rust
enum Direction {
    Left,
    Right,
}
```

Use `match` to handle each variant.

```rust
match dir {
    Direction::Left => println!("L"),
    Direction::Right => println!("R"),
}
```

`match` must cover every variant.

q2.question: Which statement about `match` on an enum is true?
options: Enums can't carry data
match needs exhaustiveness only for primitive types
match must cover every variant
All variants must share the same data type

q3.question: What does one `match` arm usually handle?
options: A single line comment
A function call only
A type annotation
One enum variant or pattern

q4.question: Why is an enum often better than a few unrelated booleans for state like direction?
options: Enums compile faster than booleans
An enum models one valid state from a finite set more explicitly
Booleans cannot be matched with `match`
Enums are the only way to store text

q5.question: What does exhaustiveness in `match` protect you from?
options: Forgetting to handle one of the possible variants
Allocating too much stack space
Using methods inside the arms
Creating references in pattern matching

09_structs.title: Structs and methods
09_structs.description:
A Rust `struct` is the closest thing to class-like data: it groups fields, but it does not bundle methods inside the type body.

```rust
struct User {
    name: String,
    age: u32,
}
```

Methods live in a separate `impl` block.

```rust
impl User {
    fn is_adult(&self) -> bool {
        self.age >= 18
    }
}
```

A useful mental map is:

```text
class-like data         -> struct
class-like methods      -> impl
interface-like behavior -> trait
variant type            -> enum
```

But the mapping is not exact. Rust structs participate in ownership rules. Assigning a struct usually moves it unless the type implements `Copy`.

q2.question: What does an `impl` block do for a struct?
options: Attaches methods and associated functions
Hides all fields from outside code
Replaces the struct's fields
Runs at program startup

q3.question: Which statement about assigning a struct value is true by default?
options: It always deep-copies every field
It usually moves the value unless the type implements `Copy`
It converts the struct into a trait object
It is only allowed inside an `impl` block

q4.question: Why is `&self` a common method receiver for structs?
options: It borrows the instance so the method can read it without taking ownership
It copies the whole struct before every call
It forces all fields to be public
It means the method is static

q5.question: Where do methods for a struct live in Rust?
options: Inside the field list
In a neighboring `impl` block
Inside the `main` function only
In the crate root automatically

10_closures_basic.title: Closures

q2.question: Which best describes `let add = |a: i32, b: i32| a + b;` ?
options: A type alias
A closure value bound to `add`
A trait implementation
A macro invocation

q3.question: Why are closures useful?
options: They can only exist inside macros
They can be stored in variables and passed around
They replace every function in Rust
They always require explicit return types

q4.question: What capability makes closures different from plain function items?
options: Closures can capture values from the surrounding scope
Closures can return values but functions cannot
Closures must always allocate on the heap
Closures are the only callable form in Rust

q5.question: Why can closure parameter types often be omitted?
options: Because closures are dynamically typed
Because the compiler can often infer them from usage
Because closures ignore their inputs
Because omitted types default to `i32`

11_closures_capture.title: Closures: capturing state

q2.question: Why must `increment` be declared `mut`?
options: Because it mutates the captured `counter`
Because all closures must be `mut`
Because `counter` is copied each call
Removing `mut` would still compile

q3.question: What is the closure using when it reads or changes `counter`?
options: A new local variable unrelated to `counter`
A macro-generated global variable
A captured value from the surrounding scope
A struct field named `counter`

q4.question: If a closure only reads a captured variable and does not mutate anything, what usually changes?
options: The closure must still be declared `mut`
The captured variable must become `static`
The closure binding often no longer needs to be `mut`
The closure stops being callable

q5.question: What does it mean for a closure to capture a value?
options: It stores or borrows access to a variable from its surrounding scope
It converts the variable into a constant
It moves every variable in the function automatically
It serializes the variable into the binary

12_iter_map.title: Iterators: map

q2.question: Which iterator method transforms each element? Fill in the blank.

q3.question: What does `collect()` do after `map(...)`?
options: Consumes the iterator and builds a collection
Sorts the iterator in place
Turns the iterator into a trait
Makes `map` run forever

q4.question: Why is iterator laziness useful with `map`?
options: It delays work until consumption, which helps composition and avoids unnecessary intermediate results
It guarantees parallel execution
It forces every iterator into a `Vec`
It prevents closures from capturing values

q5.question: What kind of items does `nums.iter()` yield for a `Vec<i32>`?
options: Owned `i32` values moved out of the vector
Mutable references by default
Shared references to each element
Tuple pairs of `(index, value)`

13_iter_filter.title: Iterators: filter

q2.question: When does `filter`'s predicate run?
options: Immediately when filter is called
Once per element, eagerly into a Vec
Lazily, only when the iterator is consumed
Filter requires a boolean array as input

q3.question: Which items does `filter` keep?
options: Items that return numbers larger than zero
Items for which the predicate returns `true`
Every item after the first one
Only items at even indexes

q4.question: What happens to an item when a `filter` predicate returns `false`?
options: The item is cloned and retried
The item is discarded from the resulting iterator
The whole iterator panics
The item is turned into `None`

q5.question: What is the main semantic difference between `iter()` and `into_iter()` on a collection?
options: `iter()` borrows items, while `into_iter()` consumes the collection to yield owned items
`iter()` sorts items, while `into_iter()` preserves order
`into_iter()` only works on arrays
There is no difference in ownership

14_iter_fold.title: Iterators: fold

q2.question: Which statement is true about `fold`?
options: It takes an initial accumulator and combines elements
It cannot produce an i32
Its initial value must always be 0
It runs in parallel

q3.question: What value is carried from one `fold` step to the next?
options: The loop index only
The input vector itself
The accumulator
A hidden mutable global

q4.question: If you call `fold(10, |acc, n| acc + n)` on `[1, 2, 3]`, what final value do you get?

q5.question: When is `fold` preferable to a helper like `sum()`?
options: When you need custom accumulation logic or a nonstandard result
Only when the iterator is empty
Only when working with strings
When you want the compiler to skip type inference

15_iter_sum.title: Iterators: sum and max

q2.question: The snippet below has one line that won't compile. Which line is it?

q3.question: What does `sum()` return when the iterator items are numbers?
options: A single total value
A new iterator of partial sums
Always a `String`
A tuple of `(sum, max)`

q4.question: Why does `sum()` often need a type annotation somewhere nearby?
options: Because iterators cannot contain numbers without annotation
Because the compiler may need help deciding the result type
Because `sum()` always returns a tuple
Because annotations turn lazy iterators into eager ones

q5.question: Why does `max()` typically return an `Option<T>` instead of just `T`?
options: Because iterators may be empty and have no maximum value
Because `max()` always allocates on the heap
Because it might need to return multiple maxima
Because `Option` is required for all numeric APIs

16_traits_basic.title: Traits

q2.question: What does `impl Greet for Person` do?
options: Defines a free function called `greet`
Provides the `Greet` trait's methods for `Person`
Removes the `Greet` trait from `Person`
Renames Person to Greet

q3.question: Why are traits useful?
options: They make all types have the same fields
They replace enums
They remove the need for methods
They let different types share the same behavior contract

q4.question: What is the main role of a trait in Rust?
options: To define a shared behavior contract that multiple types can implement
To store fields shared by several structs
To replace all generics with inheritance
To force runtime dispatch

q5.question: Why is a function parameter like `impl Greet` or `T: Greet` useful?
options: It lets the function call trait methods on different concrete types that implement the trait
It erases all type information before compilation
It means the function can only accept one exact type forever
It automatically allocates arguments on the heap

17_traits_default.title: Traits: default methods

q2.question: Why does `Dog` print `dog: ...`?
options: Both Cat and Dog override `sound()`
Dog uses the default `sound()` because it doesn't override it
Default methods cannot be overridden
Cat doesn't implement the trait

q3.question: Why are default trait methods useful in practice?
options: They force every type to use the same exact implementation
They remove the need for trait implementations entirely
They let a trait provide shared behavior while still allowing overrides
They make traits work only for enums

q4.question: When would a type override a default trait method?
options: When it needs behavior different from the shared default
Only when the trait has no required methods
Default methods cannot actually be overridden
Only inside `unsafe` code

q5.question: Why are default trait methods useful across many implementations?
options: They eliminate the need to implement the trait
They let a trait provide common behavior while allowing selective customization
They make every implementation identical and unchangeable
They work only for enums

18_traits_derive.title: Traits: derive
18_traits_derive.description:
`derive` asks Rust to generate trait implementations for you.

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone, PartialEq)]
struct Point {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
}
```

This matters for value-like structs. With `Copy`, assigning `p1` into `p2` duplicates the bits, so both variables stay usable.

```rust
let p1 = Point { x: 1, y: 2 };
let p2 = p1;
```

Without `Copy`, that assignment would move the value and `p1` could not be used afterward. `Clone` is the explicit duplicate operation when automatic copying is not available or not desired.

q2.question: What does `#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone, PartialEq)]` do?
options: Auto-generates implementations of those traits
Turns every field into a reference
Replaces the struct's fields
Runs at runtime

q3.question: Why can `p1` still be used after `let p2 = p1;` in this lesson?
options: Because `PartialEq` keeps the old binding alive
Because `Debug` prevents moves
Because the struct implements `Copy`, so assignment copies instead of moving
Because all structs in Rust are copied by default

q4.question: What is the practical difference between `Copy` and `Clone`?
options: `Copy` is explicit and `Clone` is implicit
`Copy` allows implicit duplication on assignment, while `Clone` is an explicit operation
They are exactly the same trait with different names
`Clone` only works for primitive integers

q5.question: Why is `Debug` commonly derived on structs during learning and development?
options: It enables printing values with formatting like `{:?}` for inspection
It makes the struct thread-safe
It disables move semantics
It allows pattern matching on private fields

19_generics_basic.title: Generics

q2.question: Which is the correct syntax for a bounded generic?
options: T must be a number
T = PartialOrd + Copy
T: PartialOrd + Copy
T :: PartialOrd, Copy

q3.question: Why does `largest<T: PartialOrd + Copy>` need `Copy` in this example?
options: So Rust can sort values automatically
So the function can return `a` or `b` by value without moving out of borrowed data
Because every generic type must implement `Copy`
Because `PartialOrd` only works together with `Copy`

q4.question: Why are generics valuable in Rust APIs?
options: They let one function or type work across multiple concrete types while preserving static type checking
They convert all values into trait objects
They remove the need for function signatures
They delay type checking until runtime

q5.question: What does the compiler typically do with generic code at compile time?
options: It interprets it line by line at runtime
It generates concrete versions for the types you actually use
It stores the generic body only once and skips type checking
It converts every generic into a macro

20_lifetimes_basic.title: Lifetimes

q2.question: What does the `'a` in `fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str` mean?
options: 'a is a runtime variable
The returned reference lives at least as long as both inputs
'a is required for every function
'a only applies to Strings

q3.question: What problem are lifetimes solving here?
options: They let Rust track how long returned references may safely point to input data
They make the function run longer
They allocate strings on the heap
They replace ownership with garbage collection

q4.question: What do lifetime annotations describe in Rust?
options: How long the program will run
Relationships between borrowed references so the compiler can verify validity
How many times a loop executes
Which values are allocated on the heap

q5.question: Why are lifetime annotations often needed when a function returns a reference derived from its inputs?
options: To tell the compiler which input reference the output is tied to
To make the returned reference live forever
To disable borrow checking temporarily
To force the function to allocate a new value

21_lifetimes_struct.title: Lifetimes in structs
21_lifetimes_struct.description:
A struct that stores a reference needs a lifetime parameter.

```rust
struct Borrower<'a> {
    name: &'a str,
}
```

Without `'a`, Rust would not know how long `name` is allowed to live.

q2.question: Which line below has the missing lifetime that prevents this from compiling?

q3.question: Why must `Borrower` declare a lifetime parameter?
options: Because the struct stores a reference and Rust must know how long that reference may live
Because every struct in Rust needs a lifetime
Because `String` always requires `'static`
Because structs cannot hold fields without trait bounds

q4.question: Why does a struct that stores a reference usually need a lifetime parameter?
options: To guarantee the referenced data outlives the struct value
To make the struct copyable
To allow methods inside `impl` blocks
To force the struct onto the stack

q5.question: What is one common way to avoid lifetime parameters in a struct design?
options: Store owned data like `String` instead of borrowed references when appropriate
Replace the struct with a trait object
Mark every field as `mut`
Use `unsafe` for all fields

22_result_basic.title: Result and match
22_result_basic.description:
`Result<T, E>` represents success or failure.

```rust
let value: Result<i32, &str> = Ok(42);
```

Use `match` to handle both cases.

```rust
match value {
    Ok(n) => println!("{n}"),
    Err(e) => println!("error: {e}"),
}
```

q2.question: Which is true about Result?
options: It's an enum with Ok and Err variants
It's a struct
Err always panics
match cannot pattern-match Result

q3.question: Why is `Result` preferred over panicking for recoverable failures?
options: It forces the caller to handle or propagate the failure explicitly
It makes the compiler run faster
It removes the need for return types
It guarantees the program never exits early

q4.question: What do the `Ok` and `Err` variants of `Result<T, E>` represent?
options: Two ways to borrow the same value
Success with a value, or failure with an error
Synchronous and asynchronous execution
Stack and heap allocation

q5.question: Why is matching on `Result` a useful beginner pattern?
options: It forces you to consider both the success and error paths explicitly
It automatically retries failed operations
It turns every error into a panic
It prevents functions from returning values

23_option_basic.title: Option and unwrap_or

q2.question: What does `unwrap_or("none")` do when called on `None`?
options: Panics
Returns the empty string
Returns the default "none"
Calls the function again

q3.question: When would you choose `Option<T>` instead of `Result<T, E>`?
options: When the absence of a value is expected and you do not need an error message
When you need to store two successful values
When parsing integers from text
Only when working with traits

q4.question: When is `Option<T>` usually a better model than `Result<T, E>`?
options: When absence is expected and not an error condition by itself
When you need to attach a detailed error message
When the value must always exist
When you need thread-safe sharing

q5.question: What does `unwrap_or("none")` return when the option is `Some("Rust")`?
options: `none`
A panic
`Rust`
`Some("Rust")`

24_error_propagation.title: Error propagation with ?

q2.question: Order what happens when `parse_and_double("21")` is called, in execution order.
options: Try to parse "21" as i32
Parsing succeeds; `?` unwraps Ok(21) into n
Multiply 21 by 2 to get 42
Wrap 42 in Ok and return

q3.question: What happens if the parse step returns `Err` before multiplication?
options: The function keeps going with `0`
The error is ignored
The `?` returns that error early from the function
Rust retries the parse automatically

q4.question: What does the `?` operator do when applied to a `Result` that is `Err`?
options: It converts the error into `None`
It retries the operation once
It returns early from the current function with that error
It logs the error and keeps going

q5.question: Why is `?` often preferred over nested `match` expressions for simple propagation?
options: It removes boilerplate while keeping the same success-or-early-error flow
It hides errors from the type system
It works only in `main`
It automatically recovers from all failures

25_panic_unwrap.title: panic, unwrap, expect

q2.question: What happens if `value.unwrap()` is called when `value` is `None`?
options: Returns a default
Panics at runtime
Returns Err
Returns 0

q3.question: Why is `expect("message")` often better than plain `unwrap()` during debugging?
options: It never panics
It converts `None` into `Some`
It makes the code async-safe
It panics with a custom message that explains the assumption

q4.question: What is the practical difference between `unwrap()` and `expect("message")`?
options: `unwrap()` never panics, but `expect()` does
`expect()` panics with a custom message, which can make failures easier to diagnose
`expect()` returns `Option`, while `unwrap()` returns `Result`
There is no difference at all

q5.question: When is using `unwrap()` usually a poor design choice?
options: When failure is recoverable or likely in normal operation
When working in a tiny example or test
When you are certain a value exists
When debugging code paths locally

26_async_basic.title: async / await

q2.question: What does calling an `async fn` actually do?
options: Runs the body immediately
Polls the OS scheduler
Returns a future that needs to be awaited or polled
Spawns an OS thread

q3.question: Why do we need something like `block_on` in this example?
options: Because a future does not complete by itself until an executor polls it
Because async functions cannot return values
Because Rust requires every function to be blocking
Because `await` only works inside macros

q4.question: What do you get immediately when you call an `async fn`?
options: The final return value
A running background thread
A `Future` representing work that can be polled later
A macro expansion

q5.question: What is the role of `.await` in async Rust?
options: It blocks the entire process until completion
It polls the future and suspends the async context until the result is ready
It converts a future into a thread
It turns a `Result` into an `Option`

27_box_basic.title: Smart pointers: Box

q2.question: Where does Box<T> store its data?
options: On the stack
On the heap
In thread-local storage
In a static segment

q3.question: Why would you choose `Box<T>` over a plain `T`?
options: To move the value to the heap or to build recursive structures
To share data across threads automatically
To bypass ownership checks
To make every method asynchronous

q4.question: Why is `Box<T>` especially useful for some recursive or large values?
options: It stores the value on the heap and gives the surrounding type a known, fixed size
It makes every operation on `T` asynchronous
It allows shared ownership across threads
It removes the need for dropping values

q5.question: When you have a `Box<T>`, what part stays directly in the owner variable?
options: The full `T` value inline on the stack
A pointer-like handle to heap-allocated data
A global singleton copy of `T`
Only the destructor and nothing else

28_rc_basic.title: Smart pointers: Rc

q2.question: Which best describes Rc<T>?
options: Multiple owners via reference counting
Thread-safe across threads
Deep-copies on clone
Can't hold strings

q3.question: Why is `Rc<T>` not used across threads?
options: Because its reference counting is not thread-safe
Because it can only store integers
Because cloning an `Rc` consumes the inner value
Because `Rc` always leaks memory

q4.question: What happens when you call `Rc::clone(&value)`?
options: The inner value is deep-copied every time
The reference count is increased so ownership is shared
The value is moved into a new thread
The original `Rc` becomes invalid

q5.question: When is `Rc<T>` the right tool?
options: When you need shared ownership in single-threaded code
When you need a mutable value across many threads
When you want to avoid all reference counting overhead
When you need compile-time borrow checking to be disabled

29_refcell_basic.title: Smart pointers: RefCell

q2.question: Order the steps the program performs.
options: Create a RefCell holding the value 5
Borrow mutably and add 10 to the inner value
Drop the mutable borrow when its scope ends
Borrow immutably to print the final value

q3.question: What extra risk comes with `RefCell<T>` compared with normal borrowing?
options: It disables type checking
It can panic at runtime if borrow rules are broken
It always copies the inner data
It only works with `unsafe` blocks

q4.question: What rule does `RefCell<T>` enforce at runtime instead of compile time?
options: That values are always `Copy`
That you can have either one mutable borrow or many immutable borrows
That all borrows must be `'static`
That references always point to stack data

q5.question: Why is `RefCell<T>` considered a tradeoff?
options: It enables mutation through shared access, but borrow mistakes can panic at runtime
It removes the need for ownership rules entirely
It only works with numeric types
It guarantees better performance than normal borrowing

30_macros_basic.title: Macros: built-in

q2.question: When do macros run?
options: When the binary starts up
On every function call
At compile time, expanding into code
Lazily, on first use

q3.question: Why are macros useful for things like `println!` and `vec!`?
options: They can accept flexible syntax and expand into regular Rust before compilation finishes
They run faster than machine code
They replace the type system
They only work in debug mode

q4.question: How do macros differ from ordinary functions in Rust?
options: Macros transform syntax during compilation and can accept more flexible input patterns
Macros run only after the program exits
Functions can change syntax trees but macros cannot
Macros always return `String`

q5.question: Why is `vec!` a good example of where a macro is convenient?
options: It lets you build a vector with concise syntax that would be awkward as a plain function
It is the only way to create vectors in Rust
It avoids allocating memory entirely
It makes vectors immutable by default

31_macros_custom.title: Macros: macro_rules!

q2.question: What does `macro_rules!` define?
options: A regular function
A declarative macro
An unsafe block
A trait

q3.question: How does a declarative macro decide what code to generate?
options: By matching the input against patterns and expanding the matching rule
By executing the code at runtime first
By compiling a separate crate automatically
By reading values from stdin

q4.question: What does a `macro_rules!` definition mainly contain?
options: Pattern-and-expansion rules that decide what code to generate
Only function signatures
A runtime parser for user input
A list of trait implementations

q5.question: What is one downside of heavy macro use compared with plain functions?
options: Macros cannot generate valid Rust code
Compiler errors can be harder to read because they point into generated code paths
Macros are always slower at runtime
Macros can only be used inside tests

32_unsafe_basic.title: Unsafe Rust

q2.question: Which is true about `unsafe { }`?
options: Inside it you can dereference raw pointers
It disables the type system
It makes Rust as memory-safe as Java
Raw pointers are checked at runtime

q3.question: What does `unsafe` actually change?
options: It turns off all borrow checking forever
It allows specific operations the compiler cannot verify, while the rest of Rust rules still apply
It makes raw pointers safe automatically
It removes the need for types

q4.question: Why does Rust include `unsafe` at all?
options: To allow specific low-level operations that the compiler cannot fully verify, while keeping them explicit
To disable the type system for performance everywhere
To replace modules and traits
To force manual memory management in all code

q5.question: What still remains true inside an `unsafe` block?
options: The compiler turns off all checking
Only syntax errors are reported
Most of Rust still applies; `unsafe` mainly marks operations whose extra safety conditions you must uphold
Values stop being dropped automatically

33_vec_push.title: Vectors and push

q2.question: Fill in the method used to append `3` to the vector.

q3.question: Why does the vector need `mut` before calling `push`?
options: Because `push` changes the vector's contents and possibly its length
Because vectors are immutable forever in Rust
Because `push` returns a new vector
Because all heap values require `mut`

q4.question: What does `push` do on a `Vec<T>`?
options: Inserts a new element at the front
Appends a new element to the end of the vector
Removes the last element
Sorts the vector after insertion

q5.question: Why would you choose a `Vec<T>` over an array in many programs?
options: Because a vector can grow or shrink at runtime
Because arrays cannot store numbers
Because vectors never allocate
Because vectors are always stack-only

34_option_match.title: Option with match guards
34_option_match.description:
`match` arms can use guards.

```rust
match value {
    Some(n) if n > 10 => "big",
    Some(_) => "small",
    None => "none",
}
```

The `if n > 10` part is a guard that adds an extra condition.

q2.question: Why does `Some(25)` print `big`?
options: Because every Some value is treated as big
Because the `Some(n) if n > 10` guard matches
Because match checks None first
Because Option automatically unwraps values over 10

q3.question: What is the benefit of a match guard here?
options: It lets one pattern split into more precise cases without nesting another `if`
It removes the need for `Some` and `None`
It turns `Option` into `Result`
It makes the match run at compile time

q4.question: What is a match guard in Rust?
options: A macro that wraps `match`
An extra `if` condition attached to a pattern arm
A second enum variant generated automatically
A way to skip exhaustiveness checking

q5.question: If a value matches `Some(n)` but the guard `if n > 10` is false, what happens?
options: The program panics immediately
Rust treats the pattern as unmatched and continues to later arms
The guard is ignored because the variant matched
The value becomes `None`

35_arc_mutex.title: Arc and Mutex

q2.question: Order what happens in this program.
options: Create an Arc holding a Mutex with 0 inside
Clone the Arc so two owners point to the same Mutex
Lock the Mutex and add 2 to the inner value
Lock again to print the final count

q3.question: Why are `Arc` and `Mutex` often paired together?
options: Because `Arc` shares ownership and `Mutex` protects mutable access to shared data
Because both are required for string formatting
Because `Arc` provides mutation and `Mutex` provides cloning
Because neither can be used alone

q4.question: What problem does `Arc<T>` solve in `Arc<Mutex<T>>`?
options: It provides thread-safe shared ownership of the same allocation
It makes all access automatically mutable
It removes the need for locking
It forces the value onto the stack

q5.question: What problem does `Mutex<T>` solve in `Arc<Mutex<T>>`?
options: It guarantees the value will never be dropped
It allows only one thread at a time to access the protected data mutably
It clones the value for each thread
It converts panics into `Result` automatically

36_iter_enumerate.title: Iterators: enumerate

q2.question: The snippet below has one line that won't compile. Which line is it?

q3.question: Why does `enumerate()` require a loop pattern like `(index, item)`?
options: Because `enumerate()` returns a closure
Because each iteration item is a tuple of index and value
Because Rust loops always need parentheses
Because arrays cannot be iterated without tuple syntax

q4.question: What does `enumerate()` add to an iterator?
options: Automatic sorting
A running index paired with each item
Mutable access to every item
A final total count only

q5.question: Why is `enumerate()` often preferable to managing a manual counter variable?
options: It keeps the index tied to the iterator pipeline and avoids separate counter bookkeeping
It makes loops run in parallel
It changes all items into strings
It is required for `for` loops to compile

37_thread_basic.title: Threads: spawn and join

q2.question: Why does the program call `handle.join().unwrap()`?
options: To start the thread lazily
To make the closure mutable
To wait for the spawned thread to finish
To clone the thread handle

q3.question: What does `thread::spawn` return?
options: A borrowed reference to the thread body
A `JoinHandle` that can be used to wait for the thread
A `Mutex` protecting the thread
A `Result<String, String>`

q4.question: Why is `join()` important after spawning a thread in examples like this?
options: It waits for the thread to finish before the main thread continues or exits
It moves the thread onto the heap
It makes the thread run faster
It converts the thread into async code

q5.question: Why do spawned thread closures often use `move`?
options: To transfer ownership of captured data into the thread so it can outlive the caller safely
To make the closure recursive
To force the closure to borrow everything instead
To disable synchronization

38_mutex_basic.title: Locks: Mutex

q2.question: Order what happens in this program.
options: Create a mutex holding `0`
Lock the mutex and add `5` to the inner value
Lock it again to print the final count

q3.question: Why is a `MutexGuard` useful?
options: It keeps the lock held only while the guarded value is in scope
It copies the protected data into a new thread
It removes the need to handle lock poisoning
It automatically converts shared data into `Rc<T>`

q4.question: What is a `MutexGuard`?
options: A compile-time proof that no deadlocks exist
A value representing the held lock that unlocks automatically when dropped
A background thread that watches the mutex
A macro that expands into `lock()`

q5.question: Why is it good practice to keep mutex lock scopes small?
options: To reduce contention and lower the chance of deadlock-prone designs
Because Rust forbids long lock scopes
Because small scopes make the data `Copy`
Because the guard stops working after one statement

41_trait_objects.title: Trait Objects and Dynamic Dispatch

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: What does `&dyn Draw` represent?
options: A reference to any value that implements `Draw`, using dynamic dispatch.
A macro that creates buttons.
A mutable borrow of every type at once.
explanations: Correct. The concrete type is hidden behind trait-object behavior.
It is not a macro.
That is not what trait objects mean.
q2.answerReason: Trait objects provide behavior-based polymorphism at runtime.

q3.question: How is this different from a generic `T: Draw` parameter?
options: Generics always allocate on the heap.
Trait objects can only be used in tests.
Generics preserve compile-time concrete type information, while trait objects erase it behind a runtime interface.
explanations: Generics do not always allocate.
Trait objects are a normal production feature.
Correct. This is the key design distinction.
q3.answerReason: The trait-object versus generic choice changes dispatch and what type information stays available.

q4.question: What do trait objects like `&dyn Draw` enable?
options: Storing or passing different concrete types behind one shared behavior interface
Compile-time inlining of every method call
Automatic deep cloning of values
Access to private struct fields

q5.question: What is a key tradeoff of trait objects compared with generics?
options: Trait objects require no trait implementations
Trait objects use dynamic dispatch and type erasure instead of one monomorphized concrete type
Trait objects are always faster
Generics can only work with references

44_interior_mutability.title: Interior Mutability Tradeoffs
44_interior_mutability.description:
Interior mutability allows mutation through shared ownership patterns by moving some checks to runtime or abstraction boundaries.

```rust
let value = RefCell::new(1);
```

This is useful, but it changes where borrow-rule enforcement happens.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why is `RefCell<T>` a tradeoff?
options: Because it moves some borrow enforcement from compile time to runtime.
Because it removes all ownership reasoning.
Because it is always thread-safe shared state.
explanations: Correct. Flexibility comes with different enforcement timing.
Ownership reasoning still matters.
`RefCell` is not the thread-safe shared-state default.
q2.answerReason: Interior mutability increases flexibility by changing where correctness is checked.

q3.question: What is the main design lesson?
options: Runtime borrow checks are always better.
Compile-time checks no longer matter in Rust.
Extra flexibility should be used deliberately because it changes failure modes and API expectations.
explanations: That is too absolute.
Compile-time checks still matter a lot.
Correct. The tradeoff is about where guarantees come from.
q3.answerReason: Interior mutability is powerful, but it is not a free replacement for simpler ownership models.

q4.question: What is the design appeal of interior mutability?
options: It allows controlled mutation even through shared-looking access patterns
It removes the need for borrowing rules entirely
It guarantees no runtime overhead
It works only for thread-safe types

q5.question: When is interior mutability often justified?
options: When an API is externally immutable but needs hidden state changes like caching, counters, or test doubles
Whenever you want to avoid thinking about ownership
Only for numeric algorithms
Only inside unsafe code

46_crate_boundaries.title: Crate Boundaries and Architecture

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why do crate boundaries matter beyond build organization?
options: Because only crates can contain traits.
Because modules cannot be tested.
Because they help encode API ownership, dependency direction, and isolation boundaries.
explanations: Traits are not limited to crates that way.
Modules can be tested.
Correct. Package boundaries are part of system structure.
q2.answerReason: Crate boundaries influence coupling and what invariants are enforced where.

q3.question: What is the main architecture benefit?
options: Only faster formatting.
A clearer public surface and better control over internal coupling.
Automatic removal of all unsafe code.
explanations: Formatting is unrelated.
Correct. Structure helps keep large systems disciplined.
Unsafe code is not removed automatically.
q3.answerReason: Good boundaries make large codebases easier to reason about and evolve.

q4.question: How do crate boundaries help on larger Rust codebases?
options: They encourage explicit interfaces and clearer ownership of modules between teams
They remove the need for public APIs
They guarantee zero compile times
They force every function to become generic

q5.question: Why do crate boundaries improve architecture beyond just folder organization?
options: They push you to define dependency directions and reduce accidental coupling
They make traits unnecessary
They allow circular dependencies by default
They prevent testing across crates

40_copy_clone_value.title: Copy, Clone, and Value Semantics
40_copy_clone_value.description:
A `Copy` type behaves more like a traditional value type because assignment duplicates it implicitly.

```rust
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]
struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }
```

Without `Copy`, moving the value would make the old binding unusable.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why can `p1` still be used after `let p2 = p1;`?
options: Because structs are always shared by reference.
Because `Clone` runs automatically on every assignment.
Because `Point` implements `Copy`, so the assignment duplicates the value implicitly.
explanations: That is not how ordinary struct assignment works here.
`Clone` is explicit; `Copy` is the implicit behavior.
Correct. `Copy` gives assignment-like duplication semantics.
q2.answerReason: `Copy` changes assignment from a move into an implicit duplication.

q3.question: Why does `Copy` often feel like value semantics?
options: Because `Copy` values are not stored in memory.
Because passing or assigning them does not transfer exclusive ownership away from the old binding.
Because `Copy` disables type checking.
explanations: All values are still stored somewhere in memory.
Correct. The original binding remains usable after assignment-like operations.
Type checking still applies.
q3.answerReason: Implicit duplication is why `Copy` often matches value-type intuition.

q4.question: When is `Clone` needed even if `Copy` is not available?
options: When you need an explicit duplicate of a value whose type does not allow implicit copying
When you want Rust to skip ownership checks
Only when working with integers
Only inside trait objects

q5.question: Why are many types with owned heap data, like `String`, not `Copy`?
options: Because implicit duplication of owned resources would be expensive or semantically wrong
Because heap values cannot be moved
Because `Copy` is reserved for structs only
Because `Clone` and `Copy` cannot coexist

42_send_sync.title: Send, Sync, and Thread Safety
42_send_sync.description:
`Send` and `Sync` describe what ownership and shared references may safely cross thread boundaries.

```rust
let data = Arc::new(String::from("shared"));
```

These traits shape what concurrent Rust designs are legal.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why is `Arc` used here instead of plain `Rc`?
options: Because `Rc` is always mutable.
Because `Arc` is thread-safe shared ownership, while `Rc` is not for cross-thread sharing.
Because `Arc` removes the need for ownership.
explanations: `Rc` being always mutable is false.
Correct. `Arc` is the thread-safe shared-ownership choice.
Ownership still exists.
q2.answerReason: Shared ownership across threads needs thread-safe reference counting.

q3.question: What do `Send` and `Sync` help express?
options: Whether ownership or shared references can cross thread boundaries safely.
Only how TCP packets are routed.
How the formatter aligns code.
explanations: Correct. They encode thread-safety properties.
Networking is unrelated.
Formatting is unrelated.
q3.answerReason: These traits are Rust’s vocabulary for thread-safety guarantees.

q4.question: What does it mean for a type to implement `Send`?
options: Values of the type can be transferred safely to another thread
The type can be serialized into JSON
References to the type are always mutable
The type can be pinned in memory

q5.question: What does it mean for a type to implement `Sync`?
options: The type can be copied bit-for-bit
A shared reference `&T` can be used from multiple threads safely
The type automatically uses a mutex
The type only works in synchronous code

45_async_blocking.title: Blocking Work Inside Async
45_async_blocking.description:
Async executors rely on tasks yielding cooperatively.

```rust
// blocking work here can stall executor threads
```

A blocking call in the wrong place can damage latency and throughput assumptions.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why is blocking work inside async a design problem?
options: Because async code cannot call ordinary functions.
Because blocking can starve executor threads and hurt latency or throughput.
Because blocked tasks become compile errors automatically.
explanations: Async code can still call ordinary functions.
Correct. Blocking undermines cooperative progress.
This is a runtime architecture problem, not an automatic compile error.
q2.answerReason: Async structure depends on tasks not monopolizing executor threads with blocking work.

q3.question: What is the broader lesson?
options: Concurrency model assumptions matter when choosing APIs and runtime behavior.
Only syntax matters in async Rust.
Blocking always improves fairness.
explanations: Correct. Runtime model and API choice are connected.
Syntax alone is not enough.
Blocking does not automatically improve fairness.
q3.answerReason: Architecture choices have to respect the executor’s concurrency model.

q4.question: Why is blocking work inside async code a design problem?
options: It can occupy an executor thread and delay unrelated async tasks
It turns every future into `None`
It disables `await` syntax permanently
It forces all values to become pinned

q5.question: What is usually a better approach for CPU-heavy or blocking operations in an async program?
options: Use async-aware nonblocking APIs or move blocking work to a dedicated blocking thread pool
Call `unwrap()` more aggressively
Replace `Future` with `Rc`
Add more match guards

39_shadowing_basics.title: Shadowing Versus Mutation

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: What is happening in `let x = x + 1;`?
options: The old binding is mutated in place.
A new binding shadows the previous one.
`x` becomes a macro.
explanations: Mutation would require a mutable binding assignment.
Correct. Shadowing creates a fresh binding.
`x` is still a variable binding.
q2.answerReason: Shadowing reuses the name for a new binding rather than mutating the old one.

q3.question: Why does this distinction matter?
options: Because ownership, type changes, and immutability reasoning depend on whether you shadow or mutate.
Because Rust cannot print mutable values.
Because shadowing only works in tests.
explanations: Correct. The semantics are different even when the name is reused.
Printing is unrelated.
Shadowing is a normal language feature.
q3.answerReason: Shadowing and mutation affect how you reason about the binding and its lifetime.

q4.question: What can shadowing do that ordinary mutation cannot?
options: Reuse the same name for a new binding, even with a different type
Change an immutable variable in place
Bypass all ownership rules
Modify values through shared references

q5.question: Why is shadowing often useful in transformation-heavy code?
options: It lets you keep a meaningful name as a value is refined step by step
It guarantees zero allocations
It makes values global
It replaces the need for functions

43_pin_basics.title: Pin and Stable Location
43_pin_basics.description:
`Pin` exists for abstractions that rely on values not being moved after they are pinned.

```rust
let pinned = Pin::new(Box::new(value));
```

This matters for some async and self-referential patterns.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why does `Pin` exist?
options: Because pinned values always run faster.
Because the stack cannot hold strings.
Because some abstractions rely on stable memory location for soundness.
explanations: `Pin` is not a universal speed trick.
Strings can be held by values normally.
Correct. Stable location is the point.
q2.answerReason: Pin protects designs that would be unsound if values were moved freely.

q3.question: Where does this show up often?
options: Only in comments.
In async state machines and other move-sensitive abstractions.
Only when printing macros are used.
explanations: Comments are unrelated.
Correct. Async internals are a common case.
Macros are unrelated.
q3.answerReason: Pinning becomes relevant when movement itself can break invariants.

q4.question: What problem is `Pin` designed to address?
options: Making values execute faster
Preventing certain values from being moved after they rely on a stable memory location
Sharing ownership across threads
Replacing lifetimes in all borrowed code

q5.question: Where does this concern show up especially often in modern Rust?
options: In async state machines and other self-referential patterns
Only in arithmetic code
Only inside declarative macros
In every `Copy` type
